not actually buying the rights to use the product, but rather a timebased license that grants access to the product.
The normalization of not owning what you pay for.

paragraphs.text: There are several things that concern me in the digital world. Data collection is increasing. Dark patterns are increasing. Games with mechanics that specifically targets weak individuals with tendency to gambling addiction. The normalization of not owning what you pay for. Especially video games. When games were on physical discs before the internet took off, there was a technological limitation in how game publishers could limit your access to the game. You would have the whole game, and it would work as long as you had the hardware to play it on. Now that more games are downloaded through the internet, the publishers have the technical ability to prevent you from even starting your game. And they have also legally written their way around of you not actually buying the rights to use the product, but rather a timebased license that grants access to the product. Some companies, revokes this access when they feel it's convenient for them, in order to maximize profit. This practive is absolutely unfair, and can be avoided. The argument the big game publishers puts forth, that it's too expensive to make an end of life plan for every game, is a flawed argument. Because the games industry has moved more and more towards a model where everything has to have an internet connection, they have made a framework that makes it difficult to provide and end of life plan, that would leave the game in a playable state, rather than unplayable. This reliance on internet connection, can gradually be eliminated over a span of 10 years. Many games are at risk of not working, when the publishers decide to pull the plug. Even games with a singleplayer campaign, which may have online features, but they're not strictly needed, for the game to work. Take for example the game "Trackmania" (from 2020) published Ubisoft. This game have both multiplayer and single player features. The singleplayer features does contain leaderboards. But they're not strictly needed in order to play the singleplayer levels. I enjoy the game. And I would like to still be able to play the game, if the publisher decides to stop the online servers. But this game is doomed from the beginning, being only available through the UbisoftConnect online store (even though it's purchased through Steam, which is the biggest digital game storefront in the world). So in order to not make a game with on a platform with limited life, it would be great if I could download Trackmania and other games I've paid for, just like you can on the storefront gog.com I don't expect endless support of the game. Just that the game would be left in a playable state after the publishers decides to move on. There could still be room for online only games like MMO's. But I'd really hate if ALL games were always online. Especially those who don't need to be strictly online only. Like Trackmania. Thanks.
